section.title: Jonah's Anger and God's Mercy
section.sectionRationale: The book's final movement turns from the city to the prophet's heart: Jonah's anger at God's mercy, the object lesson of the plant, the worm, and the wind, and God's closing question that the book leaves hanging. It is one unit exposing the resentment of grace.
section.thesis: The prophet is furious that God is merciful, and God answers his anger not with wrath but with a plant, a worm, and a question that presses the reader to share His compassion for a lost world.
section.frameNote: Three scenes: Jonah's angry prayer and God's question; the plant, the worm, and the wind; God's closing appeal that ends the book unresolved.

verses.text: Jonah, however, was greatly {{raah|displeased}}, and he became angry.

So he prayed to the LORD, saying, “O LORD, is this not what I said while I was still in my own country? This is why I was so quick to flee toward Tarshish. I knew that You are a gracious and compassionate God, slow to anger, abounding in {{chesed|loving devotion}}, One who {{nacham|relents}} from sending disaster.
And now, O LORD, please take my life from me, for it is better for me to die than to live.”
But the LORD replied, “Have you any right to be angry?”

units.label: The plant, the worm, and the wind

verses.text: Then Jonah left the city and sat down east of it, where he made himself a shelter and sat in its shade to see what would happen to the city.
So the LORD God {{manah|appointed}} a vine, and it grew up to provide shade over Jonah’s head to ease his discomfort, and Jonah was greatly pleased with the plant.
When dawn came the next day, God {{manah|appointed}} a worm that attacked the plant so that it withered.
As the sun was rising, God {{manah|appointed}} a scorching east wind, and the sun beat down on Jonah’s head so that he grew faint and wished to die, saying, “It is better for me to die than to live.”

units.label: God's closing question
units.note: should I not care for Nineveh?

verses.text: Then God asked Jonah, “Have you any right to be angry about the plant?” “I do,” he replied. “I am angry enough to die!”
But the LORD said, “You {{chus|cared}} about the plant, which you neither tended nor made grow. It sprang up in a night and perished in a night.
So should I not {{chus|care}} about the {{gadol|great}} city of Nineveh, which has more than 120,000 people who cannot tell their right hand from their left, and many cattle as well?”

raah.gloss: Evil, calamity, or displeasure. The very word for Nineveh's wickedness and the threatened disaster is now used of Jonah's reaction: God's mercy was, literally, a great evil to Jonah. The book turns the word back on the prophet, exposing his twisted heart.

chesed.gloss: Loving devotion, steadfast love, covenant faithfulness. Jonah quotes God's own self-description from Exodus 34:6; he knows God abounds in this love, and that is exactly why he ran, unwilling to see it poured out on his enemies.

nacham.gloss: To relent, be moved to compassion, or change a course of action. Jonah names God as One who relents from sending disaster. What draws worship from others draws only resentment from Jonah, who cannot bear that God relents toward Nineveh.

manah.gloss: To appoint, assign, or ordain. As He appointed the fish, God now appoints a plant, a worm, and a wind, three small agents to teach one stubborn man. The word shows God patiently marshalling creation to reach Jonah's heart.

chus.gloss: To pity, spare, or have compassion on. God's closing argument turns on it: Jonah pitied a plant he did not make; should not God pity a city of people He made? It is the compassion the whole book has been driving toward.

gadol.gloss: Great, large. The great city that opened the book returns at its close. Nineveh's greatness is now measured not by its threat but by the multitude of souls in it, more than 120,000, whom God will not lightly destroy.

basic.context: Nineveh is spared, and Jonah is furious. He admits this is why he ran: he knew God was merciful and did not want his enemies forgiven. He even asks to die. So God gives him a plant for shade, then a worm to kill it, then a scorching wind, and asks a pointed question: if Jonah cares this much about a plant, should not God care about a whole city of people?
basic.hermeneutics: The book ends not with a tidy resolution but with a question left hanging for the reader. Jonah's anger at grace is meant to expose our own hearts. When God asks whether He should care about Nineveh, the answer is obvious, and we are left to ask whether we truly share His heart for people we would rather see judged.
basic.originalLanguages: The word for evil, ra'ah, once used of Nineveh's wickedness, is now used of Jonah's mood: God's mercy was a great evil to him. Jonah quotes God's own words about His loving devotion, chesed, and His relenting, nacham. God appoints, manah, a plant, worm, and wind, and the climax turns on chus, to pity or spare.
basic.typology: Jonah, angry outside the city that his enemies were spared, is the dark mirror of Christ, who wept over the city and prayed forgiveness for His enemies as He was killed. The contrast throws the mercy of Jesus into sharp relief.
basic.christ: Jonah resented mercy to his enemies and wanted them dead; Jesus loved His enemies and died for them, praying, Father, forgive them. Where Jonah would not go and would not care, Christ came, cared, and gave His life. He is the true and better Jonah with a heart full of compassion.
basic.god: We learn that God is gracious, compassionate, slow to anger, and abounding in love, that He pursues even His hardened prophet with patience rather than wrath, and that He genuinely cares for people who do not yet know Him, a whole city who cannot tell right from left.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 34 is the self-description of God that Jonah quotes. Luke 15 shows the elder brother who resents mercy to the returning sinner, just like Jonah. And Luke 19 shows Jesus weeping over a city rather than gloating over its ruin.
basic.oneStory: The book ends by pressing God's heart for the nations onto every reader. God's last word is a question about compassion for a lost city, and it looks straight ahead to Christ, who does not merely pity the nations but gives Himself to save them, and to a church sent to love the very people it once feared.

context.literary: The climax and resolution that is deliberately left open. It mirrors the storm scene: again Jonah would rather die, again God asks a probing question. The plant, worm, and wind form a tight object lesson, and the book ends mid-argument, with God's question and no reply from Jonah, throwing the question to the reader.
context.historical: Jonah sits east of Nineveh, watching, still hoping for its overthrow. The detail of 120,000 who cannot tell their right hand from their left likely pictures the sheer number of people, perhaps highlighting the young or the morally ignorant, all of them objects of God's care.
context.canonical: The book's final question crowns the Old Testament's witness to God's mercy for the nations and sets up the New Testament mission. Jonah becomes a foil for Christ and a mirror for every believer tempted to resent grace shown to others.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To turn the spotlight from Nineveh's sin to the prophet's, exposing the ugliness of resenting God's mercy, and to leave the reader gripped by God's own question, so that we examine whether we share His compassion for the lost.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes Jonah's anger; it plainly does not commend it. God's closing question is the prescriptive heart: His people are to care about those who are perishing, as He does, rather than begrudge them mercy.

debates.question: Why does the book end with an unanswered question?

views.label: A deliberate appeal to the reader
views.case: By leaving Jonah silent, the writer hands the question to us: will we share God's heart for the nations? The open ending is the book's masterstroke, refusing us a comfortable resolution.

views.label: An implied rebuke of Jonah
views.case: Jonah's silence is itself the answer; he has no reply, and the reader is meant to feel the weight of his exposed and speechless resentment.

debates.bearing: Either way the ending presses the same point home: the real subject is not Jonah's fate but the reader's heart toward a God who loves those we might rather condemn.

debates.question: What is the point of the plant, the worm, and the wind?

views.label: An argument from the lesser to the greater
views.case: Jonah grieves over a plant he neither made nor grew; how much more should God, the Maker, care about a city full of living people. The lesson exposes the disproportion in Jonah's compassion.

views.label: A mirror of Jonah's misplaced values
views.case: Jonah's joy over the plant and rage at its loss reveal a heart moved by his own comfort but unmoved by thousands of souls, holding his values up for judgment.

debates.bearing: Both readings converge: the object lesson diagnoses a heart that cares more for personal comfort than for people, and calls it to be reordered after God's own compassion.

study.originalLanguages: The words expose Jonah and reveal God. Ra'ah, the evil of Nineveh and the disaster it deserved, now names Jonah's own displeasure: God's mercy is a great evil to him. He quotes God's chesed, loving devotion, and God as One who relents, nacham, the very traits he cannot forgive. God appoints, manah, a plant, a worm, and a wind, three small servants to teach one man, just as He appointed the fish. And it all lands on chus, to pity or spare: Jonah pitied a plant; should not God pity a city, the great, gadol, city that opened the book, now measured by its multitude of souls.

typology.type: Jonah angry outside the city, longing for its judgment
typology.fulfillment: The dark opposite of Christ, who wept over Jerusalem and prayed for His enemies' forgiveness, Luke 19:41 and 23:34.

typology.type: Jonah unwilling to go and to care for the nations
typology.fulfillment: Christ who comes, cares, and gives Himself for the nations, the true and compassionate prophet.

typology.type: The book's unanswered question of mercy
typology.fulfillment: Answered in the cross and the Great Commission, where God's compassion for the world is poured out and proclaimed.

study.christ: Jonah is the anti-type that magnifies Christ. Jonah resented mercy to his enemies and wanted them destroyed; Jesus loved His enemies and, from the cross, prayed, Father, forgive them, Luke 23:34. Jonah sat down to watch a city burn; Jesus wept over a city that would reject Him, Luke 19:41. Jonah would not go to Nineveh; the Son of God came all the way down to save. Where Jonah's compassion failed, Christ is the true and better Jonah, whose heart for the lost took Him to death and resurrection for the nations.
study.god: The LORD is exactly as Jonah confesses, gracious, compassionate, slow to anger, abounding in loving devotion, and relenting from disaster, Exodus 34:6. He is astonishingly patient, reasoning with an angry prophet rather than striking him down. And He genuinely cares, His final word is not about justice satisfied but about compassion for a city full of people who do not yet know their right hand from their left. This is the God who will one day give His Son for such a world.

crossReferences.note: God's self-description as gracious and compassionate, which Jonah quotes back to Him in complaint.
The elder brother resents the father's mercy to the returning son, mirroring Jonah's anger at grace.
Jesus weeps over Jerusalem, the opposite of Jonah watching for Nineveh's ruin.
The Lord is patient, not wishing that any should perish but that all should reach repentance, the heart behind God's closing question.

study.oneStory: Jonah ends with God's heart laid bare: should I not care about the great city? It is the question the whole Bible answers in Christ, who does not merely pity the nations but gives Himself to save them. The reluctant, resentful prophet is the foil that makes the compassion of Jesus shine, and the open ending sends the reader out to love the very people we might rather see judged, joining God's mission to the world.

sermon.title: Amazing Grace
sermon.why: From Begg's expository series A Study in Jonah, this message treats the close of the book, Jonah 4:5-11, the plant, the worm, and God's final question, showing the breadth of God's grace against the prophet's resentment. A clear, orthodox handling of this movement's climax.

quiz.q: Why was Jonah so angry after Nineveh was spared?

options.text: He resented God showing mercy to his enemies; it is the reason he fled in the first place.
options.note: Jonah 4:2 has him admit he fled because he knew God was gracious and would relent.

options.text: He was embarrassed that his prophecy of destruction did not come true.
options.note: Wounded pride may play a part, but the text names resentment of mercy as the root.

options.text: He was exhausted from the long journey to Nineveh.
options.note: His anger is moral and spiritual, not mere tiredness.

options.text: He was angry that God had not given him a reward.
options.note: The issue is grace to enemies, not any missing reward.

quiz.q: What was the point of the plant, the worm, and the wind?

options.text: To show that if Jonah pitied a mere plant, how much more should God pity a city of people.
options.note: Jonah 4:10-11 argues from the lesser to the greater to expose his misplaced compassion.

options.text: To punish Jonah for his disobedience.
options.note: It is teaching more than punishment; God is reasoning with him, not merely disciplining him.

options.text: To give Jonah comfort in the heat.
options.note: The plant gave brief comfort, but its removal was the lesson, not the comfort itself.

options.text: To show that God controls the weather.
options.note: God's sovereignty is assumed, but the point is compassion for people.

quiz.q: How does the book of Jonah end?

options.text: With God's unanswered question about caring for Nineveh, left hanging for the reader.
options.note: The open ending presses the question of compassion onto us, Jonah 4:11.

options.text: With Jonah repenting and rejoicing over the city.
options.note: We might hope for this, but the text leaves Jonah silent and the question open.

options.text: With Nineveh being destroyed after all.
options.note: Nineveh was spared; the tension is over Jonah's heart, not the city's ruin.

options.text: With God punishing Jonah for his anger.
options.note: God reasons with Jonah in patience rather than punishing him.

quiz.q: How does Jonah's attitude contrast with Jesus?

options.text: Jonah resented mercy to enemies and wanted them dead; Jesus loved His enemies and died praying for their forgiveness.
options.note: Jonah is the foil that magnifies the compassion of Christ, Luke 23:34.

options.text: Both struggled equally to love their enemies.
options.note: Jesus did not struggle to love enemies; He gave His life for them, unlike Jonah.

options.text: Jonah is a perfect model of Christlike mercy.
options.note: Jonah models the opposite of Christ's mercy here.

options.text: There is no meaningful contrast between them.
options.note: The contrast is stark and deliberate, throwing Christ's mercy into relief.

quiz.q: What does God's closing question reveal about His heart?

options.text: He genuinely cares about people who do not yet know Him, and longs to spare rather than destroy.
options.note: God's final word is compassion for a whole city, the heart behind 2 Peter 3:9.

options.text: He cares mainly about being proved right.
options.note: God's concern is for the people themselves, not for winning an argument.

options.text: He is indifferent to what happens to Nineveh.
options.note: The whole point is that He is far from indifferent; He cares deeply.

options.text: He values plants over people.
options.note: The argument runs the opposite way: people matter far more than a plant.

questions: Jonah admitted he ran because God was too merciful for his liking. Is there any way you resent grace being shown to people you feel do not deserve it?
Jonah cared more about a plant, and his own comfort, than about a city full of people. Where have your own comforts crowded out compassion for those who are lost?
God reasoned patiently with an angry, sulking prophet instead of striking him down. How does His patience with Jonah encourage you in your own failures and resentments?
The book ends with God's question hanging in the air, and no reply from Jonah. If God asked you that question about the people you would rather avoid, how would you answer?
Jesus is the true and better Jonah, who wept over the city and died for His enemies. How does His compassion move you to pray for and reach the very people you once feared or despised?

translationNotes.note: Greatly displeased renders a Hebrew phrase meaning it was exceedingly evil to Jonah, using the same word, ra'ah, applied earlier to Nineveh's wickedness and the threatened disaster.
Jonah quotes the ancient confession of God's character from Exodus 34:6, gracious, compassionate, slow to anger, abounding in loving devotion, chesed.
The plant is the Hebrew qiqayon, possibly the castor oil plant, as the BSB footnote notes; the same plant is meant in verses 7, 9, and 10.
Who cannot tell their right hand from their left likely pictures the vast number of people, perhaps stressing the young or the spiritually ignorant, all of them objects of God's care.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Jonah 4:1-11. Where BSB prints an em dash, in verse 2, it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
